## reference · UX Debt Tracking and Prioritization

* UX debt refers to the additional time and effort costs resulting from launching faster or easier solutions that negatively impact users, similar to technical debt.
* UX debt can accumulate in any project, regardless of the development methodology employed, and can lead to a loss of trust, traffic, and revenue if left unaddressed.
* Common contributors to UX debt include:
	+ Skipping user testing
	+ Disregarding brand standards and style guides
	+ Design by committee
	+ Misinterpreting the product vision
	+ Acquiring or merging with other products
	+ Poor communication or documentation
	+ Insufficient QA testing
	+ Legacy code or delayed refactoring
* UX debt can be found in areas such as:
	+ User interface (buttons, links, and visual styling)
	+ Interaction design (movement from page to page, animations, and scrolling effects)
	+ Copy, content, and messaging (labels, headlines, and written text)
	+ Information architecture (navigation structures and content classification)
	+ Accessibility elements (contrast, visual focus indicators, text alternatives, etc.)
	+ Customer journey consistency
	+ Omnichannel seamlessness
* To identify UX debt, get real user feedback often through:
	+ User testing
	+ Site-intercept surveys
	+ CSAT or NPS scores
	+ Issues reported by customer service
* When prioritizing UX debt, consider the severity, frequency, and location of the issues in the user's journey.
* UX debt should be tracked, managed, and paid back gradually over time to prevent users from abandoning the website or application.

Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/ux-debt/
